metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete code snippet (or cite the exact pydantic-ai 1.107.0 `AnthropicProvider.__init__` signature) in the Architecture Constraints section that proves `http_client=` / `anthropic_client=` is accepted — the current 'verified signature' claim is unsupported by any codebase evidence.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `AnthropicProvider` accepts `http_client=` / `anthropic_client=` keyword arguments as the transport-injection point, describing this as '(verified signature)' — but the only codebase evidence is `AnthropicProvider(base_url=_direct)` (runner.py:240). No test, no code citation, and no pydantic-ai API reference in the plan confirms that `AnthropicProvider` actually accepts an `http_client=` kwarg at pydantic-ai 1.107.0.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions: 'Injection is viable: AnthropicProvider accepts http_client=/anthropic_client= (verified signature)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` is a real package extra (it does not appear to be); if tenacity is needed standalone, replace AC#8's dependency reference with `tenacity` as a direct pyproject.toml dependency under the `[agents]` extra.
findings.finding: `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` is cited in AC#8 as the dependency to add for `AsyncTenacityTransport`/tenacity support, but this package extra does not appear to exist in pydantic-ai. Tenacity is a standalone library (`tenacity` on PyPI); `pydantic-ai-slim` extras are `[anthropic]`, `[openai]`, `[google]`, `[groq]`, etc. — there is no `[retries]` extra. The codebase has zero matches for this string.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria #8: 'pydantic-ai-slim[retries] is added as a dependency'
findings.suggested_fix: In `pyproject.toml` agents extra, add `'tenacity>=8'` directly rather than `'pydantic-ai-slim[retries]'`. Update AC#8 to read: 'tenacity is added as a direct dependency under the [agents] extra.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` with `tenacity>=8` (or equivalent version floor) added directly to the `agents` extra in pyproject.toml — `pydantic-ai-slim` does not publish a `[retries]` extra in v1.x and `tenacity` must be declared as a standalone dependency.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'pydantic-ai-slim[retries] must be added' as the dependency for tenacity/AsyncTenacityTransport, but pydantic-ai-slim does not publish a [retries] extra in v1.x — the package exposes extras like [anthropic], [openai], [google], not [retries]. The actual required dependency is the standalone tenacity package added directly to pyproject.toml. This is a concrete package-name MISMATCH: if an implementer runs `pip install pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` they will likely get an error or a no-op (unknown extras are typically silently ignored by pip), leaving tenacity absent at runtime.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point: pydantic-ai-slim[retries] must be added'; AC#8
findings.suggested_fix: In pyproject.toml, add `"tenacity>=8"` to the `agents` extra alongside `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic]>=1,<2`. Remove the reference to `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` from the plan and AC#8.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Downgrade the '(verified signature)' claim to '(to be verified by spike)' in the Architecture Constraints section and ensure AC#8's spike explicitly confirms both the `http_client=`/`anthropic_client=` kwarg and that a raw 429/529 HTTP response is visible to the Tenacity transport before the Anthropic SDK converts it to a typed exception.
findings.finding: The plan claims the AnthropicProvider injection-point signature was '(verified signature)' — that it accepts `http_client=`/`anthropic_client=` constructor kwargs — but no evidence of this verification appears in the codebase or plan text. The only live codebase usage of AnthropicProvider is `AnthropicProvider(base_url=_direct)` (runner.py:240) with no http_client parameter. If pydantic-ai's AnthropicProvider does not forward a custom httpx client to the underlying anthropic.AsyncAnthropic instance, the entire transport-layer retry architecture collapses. The plan's own spike requirement (AC#8) partially mitigates this, but the '(verified signature)' claim in the constraints section is stronger than the evidence supports and may mislead implementers into skipping the spike.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Injection is viable: AnthropicProvider accepts http_client=/anthropic_client= (verified signature)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a direct test path for `AsyncTenacityTransport` (e.g. an httpx `MockTransport` that returns 429/529/5xx responses with and without `Retry-After` headers) so the retry/backoff/cap behavior is verified independently of the `model_override` seam.
findings.finding: The plan's sole stated validation mechanism — 'a RED test suite that injects the full failure matrix through the pydantic-ai `model_override` seam' — operates at the pydantic-ai Model abstraction layer, which is ABOVE the httpx transport. Substituting the Model object cannot trigger `AsyncTenacityTransport`'s retry loop, backoff arithmetic, `Retry-After` parsing, or cap behavior. The novel highest-risk component (AC#2, Context 'riskiest integration point') is therefore untestable through the plan's stated test strategy.
findings.location: Success Criteria (last bullet) / AC#2 / Architecture constraints 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point'
findings.suggested_fix: In the transport-spike child story, add a standalone test that instantiates `AsyncTenacityTransport` wrapping a mock httpx transport, issues requests, and asserts: (a) 429/529/5xx are retried; (b) `Retry-After` is honored; (c) the cap is enforced; (d) 2xx passes through without retry. This is orthogonal to and does not replace the model_override RED suite.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test seam for the `AsyncTenacityTransport` layer itself (e.g., an httpx `MockTransport` that returns synthetic 429/529/5xx responses with `Retry-After` headers) so the transport-retry behavior is covered independently of the pydantic-ai model_override path.
findings.finding: The plan's RED test suite is described as injecting failures 'through the pydantic-ai `model_override` seam' (Success Criteria, AC#1). However, `model_override` operates at the pydantic-ai model abstraction layer — above the httpx transport — so it cannot exercise or validate the `AsyncTenacityTransport` retry behavior, the `Retry-After` header parsing, or the SDK `max_retries=0` enforcement. These are the novel behaviors the design introduces, yet they have no stated test seam.
findings.location: Success Criteria: 'The design is proven by a RED test suite that injects the full failure matrix through the pydantic-ai model_override seam'
findings.suggested_fix: In the transport-spike or the gate-wiring story, add a pytest fixture using `httpx.MockTransport` (or `respx`) that injects synthetic 429/5xx responses at the httpx level and asserts retry count, Retry-After honor, and max_retries=0 on the SDK.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the diagnostic-sanitization mechanism (e.g., regex pattern list, a named library, or a structured scrubber function) and add at least one RED test that injects a synthetic provider error containing a mock API key and asserts the key does not appear in any output sink (log, verdict, stderr).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a 'verbatim diagnostic' sourced from raw provider error responses that is propagated to logs, CLI stderr, session log, and the verdict — a data-flow path that crosses the Anthropic API trust boundary back into rebar's persistence/output sinks. Provider error bodies can include API key fragments, account identifiers, or auth context. The plan acknowledges sanitization is needed ('scrubbed of secrets/credentials/PII before any sink') but specifies no mechanism, pattern list, regex, or test coverage — only that an ADR will record the contract. An ADR reference without a specified sanitization design leaves this boundary-crossing path with an undeclared security contract.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Diagnostic sanitization' bullet; AC line: 'ADRs are recorded for: … the diagnostic-sanitization contract'
findings.suggested_fix: Define a `sanitize_diagnostic(raw: str) -> str` helper (e.g., in `errors.py` or a new `_sanitize.py`) that applies a small deny-list of patterns (`sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9-]+`, bearer tokens, `Authorization:` header values). Call it at the single point where `exc` is first converted to a string — runner.py:356 — so all downstream sinks receive only the sanitized form. Cover it with a parametrized unit test injecting synthetic secret-shaped strings.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete classification mapping table — specifying which HTTP status codes, SDK exception types, and structural conditions map to each of the 8 disposition values — co-located with the enum definition (e.g., in the classifier child story or a shared ADR).
findings.finding: The 8-value resolution-disposition enum (WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW, CHANGE_SETTINGS, CHANGE_INPUT, CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL, INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS, FIX_AGENT_DESIGN, NEEDS_INVESTIGATION) is referenced as a 'closed set' throughout the plan and ACs, but its value vocabulary — the mapping from error signals (HTTP status codes, exception types, SDK conditions) to disposition values — is never defined anywhere in the plan or codebase.
findings.location: Context section ('Classify what survives retries') and AC line: 'Every LLM failure is classified into the closed resolution-disposition enum + verbatim diagnostic; the classifier is total (unknown → NEEDS_INVESTIGATION) and never itself raises.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table in the classifier child story (or the gate-wiring story as single source of truth) that maps: 429 → WAIT_AND_RETRY, 529 → WAIT_AND_RETRY, 5xx transient → RETRY_NOW, 5xx persistent → NEEDS_INVESTIGATION, auth/401/403 → CHANGE_SETTINGS, context-too-long → CHANGE_INPUT, model-not-found → CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL, quota-exceeded (hard) → INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS, step-budget-exceeded → FIX_AGENT_DESIGN, everything else → NEEDS_INVESTIGATION.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `resolution_class` (and/or a `systemic_failure` boolean) as an explicit named property to the `coverage` object in each verdict schema, and specify the decision rule (which value of resolution_class triggers exit 11 vs exit 2) co-located with the gate-wiring story.
findings.finding: The plan states exit-11 vs exit-2 is 'bifurcated by the `resolution_class` carried on the verdict `coverage`,' but `resolution_class` is never defined as a field in any verdict schema (`plan_review_verdict.schema.json`, `code_review_verdict.schema.json`, `completion_verdict.schema.json`), and no processing rule specifies how the gate-wiring story reads this field to select exit code 11.
findings.location: Architecture constraints section ('Exit-code signal: Exit 11 vs the existing INDETERMINATE exit 2 is bifurcated by the `resolution_class` carried on the verdict `coverage`') and AC line: 'All three gates share the two-scope posture: systemic → block-but-retryable (exit 11, unsigned)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the 'structural guard on signing' concretely: identify its location (e.g., a precondition check in `attest.py` or `gate_dispatch.py` before `sign_manifest` is called), what condition it checks (e.g., `coverage.llm_ran == False` or `verdict == INDETERMINATE` or `resolution_class` is systemic), and which child story owns it.
findings.finding: The plan asserts a 'structural guard on signing' to ensure a degraded/INDETERMINATE verdict can never be signed/certified, but this guard is never specified: no location, no mechanism, no what-it-checks description is given anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Context section ('Never certify a degraded result (structural guard on signing)') and AC line: 'Silent-success detection + the two output self-heal tweaks are in place; a degraded/INDETERMINATE verdict can never be signed (structural guard).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 'idle-watchdog' scope between the settled-design bullet, the architecture-constraints deferral note, and AC #5: either rename AC #5's 'idle-watchdog' to 'sync-compatible liveness guard (step caps + per-request timeout)' to match what is actually in scope, or explicitly remove the deferral note and scope the async watchdog into this epic.
findings.finding: The 'idle-watchdog' is simultaneously described as IN scope (settled design: 'activity-based liveness… idle-watchdog that resets on each stream event') and OUT of scope/deferred (architecture constraints: 'The async watchdog is deferred to an ADR + follow-on ticket gated on an async-runner migration'). AC #5 then lists 'idle-watchdog' as a required deliverable without qualification, sending the implementer in two directions.
findings.location: Settled design decisions (5th bullet) vs Architecture constraints (1st bullet) vs Acceptance Criteria #5
findings.suggested_fix: In AC #5, replace 'idle-watchdog + per-request timeout + step caps' with 'per-request read timeout + step caps (sync-compatible liveness)' to match the architecture constraint that the async stream-event idle-watchdog is deferred. Add a parenthetical: '(async idle-watchdog deferred — see ADR)'. Update the settled-design bullet to match.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC item 5 with the architecture-constraints deferral decision: either reword the AC to state 'idle-watchdog deferred (ADR-N), current delivery uses per-request timeout + step caps' or explicitly split the watchdog into a separate AC line marked out-of-scope for this epic.
findings.finding: AC item 5 states 'Agentic ops use activity-based liveness (idle-watchdog + per-request timeout + step caps)' — explicitly listing the idle-watchdog as a deliverable — while Architecture Constraints state 'the idle-watchdog is NOT built now' and 'The async watchdog is deferred to an ADR + follow-on ticket'. This internal contradiction will cause implementers to either under-deliver (skip the watchdog, violating the AC) or over-deliver (build it, violating the scope deferral decision), and the ADR for deferral is not sufficient if the AC still demands the feature.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria bullet 5; Architecture constraints & verified assumptions / 'Synchronous runner' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC item 5 with: 'Agentic ops use `run_sync`-compatible liveness (per-request read timeout + per-tool timeout + step caps); the async stream-event idle-watchdog is deferred per ADR-N and tracked in <follow-on ticket>.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either update parent AC5 and SC4 to explicitly exclude the idle-watchdog (acknowledging the deferral), or add the watchdog to fd1d's scope; whichever is chosen, ensure the AC text and Architecture Constraints section are consistent.
findings.finding: Parent AC5 and SC4 require the idle-watchdog as a delivered component ('activity-based liveness (idle-watchdog + per-request timeout + step caps)'; 'caught by the idle-watchdog + step caps'), but child fd1d explicitly defers the stream-event idle-watchdog to a follow-on ADR, and no other sibling delivers it. The parent AC text was not updated to reflect the deferral stated in the Architecture Constraints section, creating an internal contradiction where the AC asserts a conjunction that no child fulfills.
findings.location: child fd1d-5f0d-4bd8-40f4; parent AC5, SC4
findings.suggested_fix: Update parent AC5 to read: 'Agentic ops use activity-based liveness (per-request read timeout + per-tool timeout + step caps); the stream-event idle-watchdog is deferred (ADR + follow-on) and is NOT part of this epic's delivery.' Update SC4 analogously. fd1d's scope and AC already correctly describe the deferral; the parent criteria are the mismatched artifact.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a dedicated spike or RED-test fixture that injects a hung/stalled LLM call into a `run_sync` gate and verifies the per-request timeout + step-cap combination terminates it within the expected bound (validating the `run_sync`-compatible liveness claim before it is asserted as proven).
findings.finding: The `run_sync`-compatible liveness design (per-request read timeout + step caps, no total-runtime timeout, no async idle-watchdog) is asserted sufficient to catch zombie runs, but no dedicated spike or named RED-test scenario verifies this combination prevents a genuinely stalled `run_sync` call from hanging indefinitely. The failure-matrix RED suite is described generically ('injects the full failure matrix') without explicitly naming hung/stall scenarios as a covered case.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions / 'Synchronous runner' bullet; Success Criteria bullet 4; AC bullet 5
findings.suggested_fix: In the gate-wiring or liveness child story, add a named fixture using `model_override` (or a mock httpx transport that sleeps) to assert that a `run_sync` call with a deliberate hang raises/times-out within N seconds; record the empirically chosen timeout value in config.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between the 'no total-runtime timeout' constraint (AC#5 / activity-based liveness bullet) and the 'wall-clock timeout' in the silent-success layer bullet: either clarify that 'wall-clock timeout' refers only to the per-request read timeout (already specified), or explicitly scope it (e.g. 'per-request wall-clock timeout, not a gate-level total-runtime timeout').
findings.finding: The plan contains a contradictory specification: the 'Activity-based liveness' settled design bullet and AC#5 both assert 'no total-runtime timeout exists in the gate path,' yet the 'Silent-success layer' design bullet lists 'wall-clock timeout' as a detection mechanism. An implementer cannot simultaneously satisfy both directives without knowing what scope the 'wall-clock timeout' covers (per-request? per-idle-window? something else entirely?).
findings.location: Settled design decisions – 'Activity-based liveness' bullet; 'Silent-success layer' bullet; AC#5
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'wall-clock timeout' in the silent-success layer bullet with 'per-request read timeout (already specified in the activity-based liveness design)' to make clear this is the same mechanism, not an additional gate-level total-runtime cap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the refusal-as-content detection protocol: specify the signal(s) to detect (e.g., summary/finding text matching a refusal-phrase list, empty findings with a non-empty refusal-keyed summary), the routing decision (which error class is raised or which disposition is assigned), and which child story owns this detection.
findings.finding: The plan names 'structured refusal-as-content detection' as part of the silent-success layer but provides no detection protocol: no definition of what patterns/field values constitute a content-level refusal (as distinct from SDK-level `stop_reason=refusal` already handled by `check_stop_reason`), no routing decision rule, and no schema field for the result.
findings.location: Context section ('Silent-success layer: wall-clock timeout, verdict schema-shape check, structured refusal-as-content detection, and build-time capability/config lints') and AC line: 'Silent-success failures (refusal-as-content, empty/truncated-looks-complete, silent hang) are detected and routed, not returned as a bogus PASS/BLOCK.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC #4 and Success Criterion #4 to name only the sync-compatible liveness mechanisms (per-request read timeout + per-tool timeout + step caps) that are in scope NOW, and explicitly reference the deferred async watchdog ADR ticket number/name rather than listing it as an in-scope deliverable.
findings.finding: AC #4 / Success Criterion #4 require 'idle-watchdog' but the Architecture Constraints section explicitly defers the async idle-watchdog to a follow-on ticket; an executor reading the ACs in isolation will attempt to build the async watchdog now, contradicting the stated deferral.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria #4 ('Agentic ops use activity-based liveness (idle-watchdog + per-request timeout + step caps)') vs Architecture Constraints ('The async watchdog is deferred to an ADR + follow-on ticket')
findings.suggested_fix: AC #4: 'Agentic ops use activity-based liveness (per-request read timeout + per-tool timeout + step caps); the async stream-event idle-watchdog is deferred to [ADR/ticket ref] and is NOT part of this epic. No total-runtime timeout exists in the gate path.' Mirror this wording in Success Criterion #4.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/runner.py` to 2eca's files scope (or designate a different child as the runner.py integrator) and define a clear merge/conflict-resolution protocol for all five concurrent edits.
findings.finding: Five children (30ff, 95a5, fd1d, 978d, 9a82) all modify `src/rebar/llm/runner.py` at overlapping seams (exception handling, transport wiring, timeout config, construction-time checks, and output validation), but child 2eca — the named integration owner — does not include `runner.py` in its stated files scope (`gate_dispatch.py`, `transition_close.py`, `schemas/`, `docs/`). This creates an unowned multi-party merge coordination gap for the most-contested file in the epic.
findings.location: child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38 (Scope and Files sections); runner.py edits in children 30ff, 95a5, fd1d, 978d, 9a82
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add runner.py to 2eca's scope with an explicit note that 2eca performs the final runner.py integration pass, or (b) assign runner.py sub-sections to named owners (e.g., 95a5 owns lines 238-245 transport block; 30ff owns lines 349-361 except block; fd1d owns timeout config injection) with a merge checklist in the parent plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the intra-parallel-group dependency: 30ff must be code-complete before 978d's routing logic is tested end-to-end; 2eca's schema must be merged before 978d's schema-shape check is validated against the full schema.
findings.finding: Child 978d has an implicit sequential dependency on both child 30ff (resolution_class enum, for routing detected silent-success failures) and child 2eca (verdict schema with resolution_class/diagnostic fields, for schema-shape validation), but all three are in the same parallel delivery group {2,3,4,5}. The 978d AC promises 'detected and routed, not returned as a PASS/BLOCK verdict' — routing requires resolution_class from 30ff. If 978d lands before 30ff, its routing is incomplete and tests may pass against stub behavior that breaks on integration.
findings.location: child 978d-1d5d-8287-47bc (Schema coordination note, AC); child 30ff-21a3-0724-422b; child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit dependency note to 978d: 'Depends on 30ff for LLMOutcome/resolution_class and on 2eca for the final verdict schema shape; integration tests for routing must be run after both are merged.' Alternatively, move 978d to position 5 (after 30ff at position 2) within the parallel group's integration sequence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit task (in the gate-wiring story or a docs-cleanup child) to audit and update/retire all existing references to `LLMUnavailableError`, 'opaque single-error collapse', and 'exit 2 for systemic failure' in docs, runbooks, and inline comments.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new resolution-disposition taxonomy (WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW, CHANGE_SETTINGS, etc.) that operators and on-call engineers will use to interpret failures, but does not address whether existing runbooks, operator guides, or error-message references that mention `LLMUnavailableError` or 'exit 2 for systemic failure' will be updated or marked stale.
findings.location: Success Criteria; Acceptance Criteria — 'Docs cover the taxonomy…'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly assign children to merge-order positions 0 and 1 in the parent plan (recommend: a879=0, 95a5=1) so the delivery sequence is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The stated merge order '0→1→{2,3,4,5}→6' assigns children to two sequential positions (0 and 1) before the parallel group, but no child is explicitly identified as position 0 or position 1. Given the parent's explicit statement that the transport-injection spike (95a5) must be de-risked 'before dependent work builds on it,' 95a5 should clearly be at position 1 (or 0). Similarly, a879 (RED baseline) must pin current behavior before any story changes that behavior — it should be position 0. The ordering ambiguity risks behavior-changing stories (30ff, fd1d, 978d) landing before the baseline is established or the spike is proven.
findings.location: parent 'Architecture constraints & verified assumptions'; child a879-3b64-1be9-4035; child 95a5-41eb-cffd-477b
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the parent plan: 'Position 0: a879 (RED baseline, must land before any behavior change). Position 1: 95a5 (transport spike, must be proven before parallel group begins implementation). Positions 2–5: 30ff, fd1d, 978d, 9a82 (parallel). Position 6: 2eca (integrator, lands last).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a section or AC enumerating every caller that currently catches/inspects `LLMUnavailableError` (CLI entry points, test fixtures, integration harnesses) and explicitly state whether each is updated or deprecated as part of this epic.
findings.finding: The plan changes the existing `LLMUnavailableError` error surface (classifying it into a disposition enum, routing systemic failures to exit 11) but does not enumerate which callers currently catch or branch on `LLMUnavailableError` by name, leaving them as potentially silently broken after the change.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions / Context section ('today every LLM failure collapses into one opaque LLMUnavailableError at runner.py:349-361')
findings.suggested_fix: Add an 'Affected callers' subsection under Architecture Constraints listing each site (e.g., `runner.py` catch block, CLI `main()`, any test using `pytest.raises(LLMUnavailableError)`) with a one-line migration note (e.g., 'updated to catch ResolutionDisposition.NEEDS_INVESTIGATION' or 'removed; exit-code check replaces exception check').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either enumerate the 7 child stories (at minimum by title/slug) in this epic document, or replace the count-based AC #1 with a content-based criterion that lists the specific deliverables each child must cover (transport spike, classifier, gate-wiring, liveness, silent-success, signing guard, ADRs).
findings.finding: AC #1 commits to 'All 7 child stories are delivered' but nowhere in this epic document are the 7 stories enumerated, named, or cross-referenced with IDs. The criterion cannot be verified from this document.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria #1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default Retry-After cap value (e.g. 'capped at 60 s') directly in AC #2 or the Architecture Constraints section so the executor has a concrete number to implement and test against.
findings.finding: 'Retry-After is honored and capped' appears in AC #2 but no cap value (or even a range/default) is stated anywhere in the plan, forcing the executor to choose an arbitrary number.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria #2 ('Retry-After is honored and capped')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC #2: '…honored and capped at 60 s (configurable via `llm.retry_after_max_seconds`)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State default values for per-request read timeout and step cap (e.g. 'read timeout: 120 s; step cap: 50 steps') in the Architecture Constraints or a config-defaults table, so the executor has concrete numbers to implement and tests can assert on them.
findings.finding: 'Generous per-request read timeout' is used in the Architecture Constraints section as a design decision but no value or range is given, leaving the executor to choose arbitrarily.
findings.location: Architecture Constraints ('generous per-request read timeout')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Default liveness parameters' row to the Architecture Constraints: 'per-request read timeout: 120 s; per-tool call timeout: 30 s; max steps: 50 — all overridable via config.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in one place (e.g., the settled-design bullet or a terminology note) that `resolution_class` is the attribute name and `ResolutionDisposition` (or whatever the enum is called) is the type, and that 'resolution-disposition' is the informal name for both.
findings.finding: The plan uses both 'resolution-disposition' (as a noun phrase for the enum) and 'resolution_class' (as the attribute name on the verdict coverage object) without ever equating them. A reader cannot tell whether these are the same concept under two names or two distinct fields.
findings.location: Settled design decisions (2nd bullet) vs Architecture constraints (4th bullet 'Exit-code signal')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single sentence: "The enum type will be called `ResolutionDisposition`; the field on the verdict coverage object is `resolution_class: ResolutionDisposition`.", and use one consistent label ('resolution disposition' or 'resolution_class') throughout the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the exit-code contract change (e.g., a feature flag or expand-contract phase where exit 11 is emitted alongside exit 2, with a deprecation window), not just an ADR acknowledgement.
findings.finding: Exit code 11 is a CLI contract change that external consumers (CI pipelines, scripts, monitoring integrations) depend on, but the plan records only an ADR acknowledgement — it provides no rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing. Per the rubric, acknowledgement alone is insufficient; the remedy must include the reversibility mechanism.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Exit-code signal' bullet; Acceptance Criteria line 'ADRs are recorded for: the exit-11-vs-INDETERMINATE contract'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a transition phase: emit both exit 11 and write a structured field in the verdict JSON so consumers can migrate to reading the field; keep exit 2 as fallback for one release cycle, then cut over. Document the cutover point in the ADR and the gate-wiring story's acceptance criteria.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly acknowledge `LLMUnavailableError`'s removal/replacement as a breaking change, enumerate known catch-sites, and add either an expand-contract phase (keep the old type as an alias/subclass for one release) or a codebase-wide migration task with a rollback note in the relevant child story.
findings.finding: The plan replaces the current opaque `LLMUnavailableError` (runner.py:349-361) with a richer classification system, but does not acknowledge this as a breaking change for any code or external consumer currently catching or matching on `LLMUnavailableError`, nor does it provide a migration path or rollback.
findings.location: Context section — 'src/rebar/llm/runner.py:349-361'; no migration section exists in the plan

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (or record as an explicit decision) that the infrastructure-reliability cluster (retry/liveness/transport) and the output-correctness cluster (silent-success/signing-guard) are intentionally co-epic'd because their coupling through the shared resolution-disposition model and gate-posture makes splitting them more costly than keeping them together — and capture this rationale in the 'scope-vs-title breadth' ADR.
findings.finding: The epic conflates two distinguishable 'reasons to change': (A) infrastructure reliability (transport retry, activity-based liveness, step caps, Retry-After handling) and (B) output correctness/quality (silent-success detection, refusal-as-content, schema-shape check, output self-heal, signing guard). These clusters could be owned by different personas (platform/infra vs. product/quality) and have independent change drivers. The plan itself acknowledges 'scope-vs-title breadth' as an ADR item, signalling the author's own uncertainty. Each cluster is plausibly independently valuable and testable in isolation.
findings.location: Epic overview / Success Criteria / Architecture constraints / AC list — the plan groups transport retry + liveness + silent-success + signing guard under one epic
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'scope-vs-title breadth' ADR, explicitly document: (1) why the two clusters are one epic (shared resolution-disposition enum, shared gate posture, shared gate-wiring integrator), and (2) the decision criteria that would trigger splitting them in a future refactor. This converts an implicit scope decision into a reviewable record without changing the plan's structure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly evaluate `httpx`'s built-in transport retry and/or `stamina` as alternatives to a custom `AsyncTenacityTransport`, and record the rejection rationale (e.g., 'httpx retry does not honor Retry-After'; 'stamina doesn't support async transport injection') in the Architecture Constraints or an ADR.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel `AsyncTenacityTransport` httpx-transport pattern as the single retry layer, but does not evaluate the directly relevant established alternative: httpx's own `httpx.AsyncHTTPTransport` with retry support (via `httpx.Retry` / `httpcore` transport retries), or the `stamina` library (tenacity wrapper purpose-built for resilient Python services). The plan cites pydantic-ai/LangGraph/Instructor/Claude Agent SDK but not the httpx-native retry mechanism it is being layered on top of.
findings.location: Context / Architecture constraints: 'Self-healing retry at the httpx transport layer (AsyncTenacityTransport)'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
